source: Graham & Doddsville (Columbia Business School) — Investor interview with Gavin Baker, CIO of Atreides Management (Issue 43, Fall 2021)
summary: Technology investing has a durable 10-20 year cycle structure; cloud/internet cycle still has legs
summary_extended: Baker argues that technology investing is characterized by durable 10-to-20-year cycles in which old paradigms do not disappear but instead accrete in layers alongside new ones — he cites IBM still selling mainframes as evidence that prior cycles persist. He traces the pattern from the 1990s client-server cycle through the internet era, noting that the internet took nearly a decade to fully manifest in core enterprise technology via cloud adoption. His structural view is that the current cloud and AI infrastructure cycle is similarly long-duration, meaning investors who maintain sector exposure over multi-year horizons benefit from compounding advantages rather than needing to time rotations. The investment implication is a general long-term commitment to the cloud/AI theme rather than any specific near-term trade, with no precise price targets or timeframes cited beyond the broad 10-20 year historical analog.
detail: Baker argues tech rarely disappears but accretes in layers (IBM still sells mainframes). The 1990s client-server cycle lasted a decade; the internet took nearly 10 years to affect core tech via cloud. These long-duration cycles create compounding advantages for investors who stay in the sector. The implication is that the current cloud/AI cycle is similarly durable.
confidence_evidence: Baker presents this as a historical structural observation used to frame his long-term sector commitment, not a specific near-term trade
quote_fragment: there do tend to be these very durable 10-to-20-year cycles in tech

ticker_audit.reasoning: Baker's thesis is a macro structural observation about tech cycle duration, not a specific claim about Amazon. AWS is a cloud leader but Baker does not name or specifically imply AMZN in this thesis; adding it is speculative extrapolation from the theme.
Same issue as AMZN — MSFT is a major cloud player but Baker makes no specific reference to Microsoft in this structural cycle thesis. Its inclusion is theme-bleed, not a direct implication.
GOOGL is tangential for the same reasons; Baker's historical cycle framing does not specifically implicate Google Cloud or Alphabet.
Snowflake is a cloud-era beneficiary but Baker does not reference it in this thesis. Its inclusion appears to be contamination from adjacent theses in the same transcript.
Salesforce is a SaaS/cloud company but Baker's structural cycle observation does not specifically implicate CRM. This is theme-bleed from the broader cloud discussion.
